Commandant Jeremiah (Jer) O'Leary — Kate O'Leary's Brother
Commandant Jeremiah O'Leary — Diarmuid Ó Laogáire
Born 1894, Mount, Scartaglin, Co. Kerry · Killed 29 May 1923, Castleisland, Co. Kerry · Aged 29
Parents
James O'Leary & Hannah Scanlan, Mount, Scartaglin. Only son and eldest child. Kate O'Leary's brother — and therefore Alice O'Connor's uncle, and Ger O'Keeffe's great-uncle.
Education & Character
A bright pupil at Scartaglin National School and an avid reader with a keen interest in scientific books. Later worked on the family farm. His four sisters were members of Cumann na mBan.
IRA Service
A Volunteer in the Scartaglin Company from its foundation — attached to the 1st Battalion, Kerry No. 2 Brigade. Came to prominence in the Black and Tan War. Participated in the attack on the barracks in Scartaglin on 31 March 1920. Appointed O/C of the 1st Battalion in May 1921, commanding companies from Castleisland, Scartaglin, Cordal, Currow, Lyre, Knocknagoshel and Brosna.
Reorganisation & Final Command
In July 1921, his battalion was transferred to Kerry No. 1 Brigade and redesignated the 7th Battalion. Jer O'Leary was appointed V/C to Brigade O/C Humphrey Murphy. A fearless and popular soldier, he led his men in engagements at Scartaglin, Brosna, Glenlahern, The Black Banks, Feale's Bridge and on the streets of Castleisland.
Civil War & Death
Jer O'Leary and his 7th Battalion remained loyal to the Republican cause during the Civil War. The battalion lost thirteen men killed in action or murdered in custody from 1922 to 1924. He had a dugout built beneath the outer stairs of a large three-storey house owned by the Walsh family of Cordal. On 24 May 1923, Frank Aiken ordered IRA units to dump arms and a ceasefire was declared. On 28 May, Jer met fellow officer Seán Kerins at Kilmurry House, Cordal. A large Free State force surrounded the house. Outnumbered and with civilians present, Jer and Kerins surrendered. He was taken to Free State headquarters at the fortified Hartnetts Hotel, Castleisland.
Killed in Custody
At 11.30 a.m. on 29 May 1923, Jer was brought to the barracks yard by Captain O'Donnell. He was ordered to close a gate that had been conspicuously left open. As he approached it, he was shot in the back by Lieutenant Patrick McGinn and Captain Patrick Byrne. Captain Martin Nolan of the Dublin Guard fired twice more as he lay on the ground. A woman passing outside held his head in her arms as he died. Local shopkeeper Con Browne brought the news to the O'Leary family that their only son had been shot dead. Jer O'Leary was 29 years old. The killing was witnessed and a statement subsequently given to the newspaper Éire, 7 July 1923.
Burial, Memorials & Legacy

Commandant Jeremiah O'Leary was buried in the Republican Plot, Kilbannivane Cemetery, near Castleisland. His name is inscribed on the Republican memorial there amongst the men of his command. His father James later bought a family grave adjacent to where his son was buried; when Hannah Scanlan died in 1934, Jer's name was inscribed on the family headstone also.

Today a plaque on the wall of Hartnetts Hotel — now the Castle Bar — marks the spot where Commandant Jeremiah O'Leary was killed by his Free State captors. Near his home in Scartaglin, a large Celtic cross was erected to his memory by public subscription and unveiled in May 1946. On it his name is inscribed in his native language: Diarmuid Ó Laogáire.

On 29 May each year until her own death, his sister Kate O'Connor took out the scarf that her brother wore when he was killed — and shed a tear.
The Military File — James O'Leary's Fifteen-Year Struggle

Following Jer O'Leary's killing, his father James pursued a pension claim on behalf of the family under the Army Pensions Acts. The resulting file — held in the Irish Military Archives — spans fifteen years of correspondence, from 1933 to 1938, and is both an important military history document and a deeply human one. It contains formal service certifications, political petitions, an official investigation report, and James O'Leary's own letters written directly to the Taoiseach. Taken together, these records give the fullest picture we have of Jer O'Leary's service and of his father's grief.

Official Military Certification — Military Service Registration Board
Certification
Two separate service certificates (dated May 1934 and March 1938) formally certified that Jeremiah O'Leary was a member of Óglaigh na hÉireann from 1916 until his death on 29 May 1923, and that he was engaged in qualifying military service throughout that period.
Role
Battalion O.C. / Vice-Commandant, Kerry Nos. 1 and 2 Brigades. Commanding officer: Humphrey Murphy.
Manner of Death
"Deceased was killed by Free State Forces at Castleisland, Co. Kerry, on 29th May, 1923, while a prisoner." No wound, injury, or disease claim was attached — the sole basis for the claim was death in service.
Gratuity Awarded
£112 10s. — one hundred and twelve pounds and ten shillings — recommended by the Army Pensions Board (chaired by John McCoy) in June 1934, noting the "very special circumstances" of the case. An internal note by a senior official (P.Ó Muircadha, 9 July 1934) argued that dependency "must be admitted" given that Jeremiah was an only son. Approved and forwarded to the Dept. of Finance, 16 July 1934.
James O'Leary's Letters — A Father's Voice
The pension file contains letters written by James O'Leary himself, at a time when he was in his seventies, living away from his own farm. In March 1933, he submitted his first application, stating that Jeremiah was his only son and that he had "relied principally on him for the management of my farm."

In July 1938, he wrote directly to the Taoiseach's office in a personal letter of remarkable force. He reminded the government that in 1926 he had been told "things would come right yet" and that they would see after "the likes of me." He described Jeremiah as Vice O.C. of Kerry No. 1 Brigade — "a wanted man. Being a good organiser and a fighter that was felt." He explained that the £112 10s. gratuity had been entirely consumed: his wife Hannah had been sick before she died, and "between doctors' fees, funeral costs, and Masses for her soul" the money was gone. He described his son-in-law making things "very unpleasant" for him so that he had been forced to leave his own house and was now living on a 10-shilling-a-week old age pension at Cordal East, Castleisland, in the house of his son-in-law Michael O'Loughlin. He ended bluntly: "I will accept a bulk sum, but I will not take a paltry sum — his loss to me and my family is great."

In December 1938, he wrote again, describing Jeremiah as having been "away from me from the start of the Tan War until his death in 1923 when he was shot in barracks after his arrest." He was still seeking justice at over 78 years of age.
Eyewitness Account — Collecting the Body
Among the later pages of the military file is a handwritten letter — from James O'Leary or a close associate — giving a personal account of the night of the Rising, Jeremiah's enlistment, and the aftermath of his killing. The writer described being brought to Castleisland to collect the body:

"The military would not leave my women dress him, they did it themselves — I suppose they didn't want us to see the state he was in when we brought him to Castleisland."

He named two comrades — Peter Browne of Scartaglin and John Kevin of Cortglass, Scartaglin — as people who could corroborate events and who were still alive at the time of writing. The enlistment is described with striking simplicity: "In 1916 the night of the Rising he called at my room-door."
The Political Campaign & Investigation
Before the first claim could even be processed, a petition signed by James O'Leary and around sixteen other Kerry families — all dependants of men killed in the Anglo-Irish War or Civil War — was sent to President Éamon de Valera via Fred Crowley T.D., demanding immediate action under the Army Pensions Act 1932. The Taoiseach's office forwarded it to the Minister for Defence (Frank Aiken), whose secretary confirmed in June 1933 the cases would be "specially attended to."

A Customs & Excise Investigation Officer at Newmarket, Co. Cork filed a report in August 1938 in response to the second application. It recorded that James was over 70, had three daughters (aged approximately 43, 41 and 38 — all married to farmers), and that the family farm had been assigned to a daughter on her marriage. David McCarthy of Breahig, Castleisland — a neighbour who had first written on James's behalf in February 1933 — described Jeremiah simply as his only son, "murdered while a prisoner in Castleisland Barracks in May 1923."
Consequence for the O'Leary Farm — Why Kate Inherited

Jeremiah was the only son of James O'Leary and Hannah Scanlan, and in the custom of the time would have been expected to inherit the 87-acre farm at Mount, Scartaglin. His killing at 29, unmarried and without children, meant that the farm passed instead to the surviving daughters. Kate O'Leary — who had married David O'Connor in February 1925, just two years after Jer's death — became the inheriting child, and the 1926 census records David O'Connor living at the farm and assisting James O'Leary as his son-in-law. The farm's inheritance by Kate and David directly shaped the circumstances into which their daughter Alice O'Connor (mother of Ger O'Keeffe) was born and raised.

The pension file makes this consequence explicit: by 1938 James himself had been forced off the farm, was living as a guest in his son-in-law's house at Cordal East, and was surviving on ten shillings a week. The son whose death had made all of this inevitable had been killed fifteen years earlier at the age of 29.
Section 5
The Curtin Family
Killaculleen & Tournafulla, Co. Limerick · c.1820 to present

This section documents the Curtin family of Killaculleen, Tournafulla, Co. Limerick — the maternal family of Catriona (Katie) Curtin. The line runs from Laurence John Curtin (b.c.1820, Barna, Co. Limerick) through his son Maurice Laurence Curtin, then to Daniel Curtin and Bridget (Babe) Kissane, whose daughter Noreen Curtin (b.1939) married Michael Curtin in 1961. Noreen and Michael are the parents of Catriona (Katie) Curtin, wife of Ger O'Keeffe. Note: both sides of Katie's family carry the Curtin name — this section covers Noreen's Curtin line (Killaculleen); Section 6 covers Michael's Curtin line (Glenquin South).

Generation Tree · Section 5
Origins — Laurence John Curtin & Johanna O'Brien
Laurence John Curtin & Johanna O'Brien
Laurence born c.1820, Barna, Co. Limerick · Died 1878, Barna, Co. Limerick, aged ~58
Role
First recorded generation of this Curtin line. Laurence John Curtin and Johanna O'Brien are the great-great-grandparents of Catriona (Katie) Curtin on her mother's side.
Children (known)
Maurice Laurence Curtin (b.1851, Barna — see below).
Maurice Laurence Curtin — Two Marriages
Born 1851, Barna, Co. Limerick · Died 1925, Killaculleen, Tournafulla, Co. Limerick, aged 73–74
1st Marriage — Johanna Begley, 1877
Married Johanna Begley (b.1853, Monagea; daughter of Cornelius (Con) Begley) at the Church of Our Lady of the Visitation, Monagea, Co. Limerick, 1877. Witnesses: Lawrence L Curtin & Thomas Roche. Johanna died 1886, Killaculleen, aged 33, leaving six young children.
2nd Marriage — Margaret O'Mahony, 1899
Married Margaret O'Mahony (daughter of Thomas O'Mahony) at Ashford Church, Co. Limerick, 1899. Margaret was born in Ashford, Co. Limerick, and died at Killaculleen, Tournafulla.
Children of Maurice Laurence Curtin & Johanna Begley
By Johanna Begley (1st wife, married 1877, died 1886)
NameBornDiedNotes
Johanna (Joe) Curtin1878, Killaculleen—Married Daniel O'Mahony. Children: Johanna Angela, Oona Teresa, Thomas Gerard, Bridget Pauline, Margaret Mary, Maurice Lawrence (d.1921 infant).
Laurence (Larry) Curtin1879, Killaculleen——
Cornelius Curtin1880, Killaculleen1938, KillaculleenSingle. Living at home in 1911 census with father and stepmother. Present in 1926 census as brother assisting on farm.
John (Jack) Curtin1881, Killaculleen——
Jeremiah (Jerome) Curtin1883, KillaculleenDied in England—
Daniel Curtin1885, Killaculleen1946, Killaculleen, aged 60–61Married Bridget (Babe) Kissane, 1925. Father of Noreen Curtin. See below. Buried Old Churchtown Cemetery, Newcastlewest.
Daniel Curtin & Bridget (Babe) Kissane
Daniel Curtin & Bridget (Babe) Kissane
Daniel born 1885, Killaculleen · Died 1946, Killaculleen · Bridget born 1894, Kilcox, Lisselton, Co. Kerry · Died 1939, Killaculleen, aged 44–45
Daniel's Parents
Maurice Laurence Curtin (1851–1925) & Johanna Begley (1853–1886), Killaculleen, Tournafulla.
Marriage
1925 — St. Mary's Church, Asdee, Ballylongford, Co. Kerry. Daniel was approximately 40, Bridget approximately 31.
Burial
Both buried at Old Churchtown Cemetery, Newcastlewest, Co. Limerick.
IRA Service & Pension
Daniel served in the Tournafulla Company, Irish Volunteers from 1915, rising to Battalion Adjutant, 2nd Battalion, West Limerick Brigade, IRA. He was awarded a Military Service Pension at Grade D in 1935 for two years of qualifying service during the War of Independence (1920–1921). Military Archives reference: MSP34REF344.
Bridget's Parents
John Richard (John R) Kissane & Hanora Buckley, Kilcox, Lisselton, Co. Kerry. Bridget was a Kerry woman who came to Tournafulla on her marriage.
Note on the 1926 Census
Daniel is recorded as Head of the Killaculleen household in the 1926 census (Schedule 54–5), Tournafulla, with his wife Bridget and infant daughter Joan. His unmarried brother Cornelius (47) is present assisting on the farm of 156 statute acres. Two farm labourers and two female servants are also recorded.
Confirmed Death Date
Daniel died in 1946 — confirmed by a pension payment envelope returned to the Dept. of Defence from Killaculleen, stamped "Deceased," postmarked 30 September 1946 (file 34D92). He was still receiving his £20 per annum pension until shortly before his death. Exact date not recorded on file.

Enumerator: Edward Sheehan. 156 statute acres. The infant Joan Curtin (b.1926) is the eldest child of Daniel & Bridget — she would later marry John Hennessy in 1956.
Daniel Curtin — IRA Service & Military Pension

Daniel Curtin joined the Tournafulla Company of the Irish Volunteers in 1915, elected as Lieutenant by the votes of the men themselves — when Ernest Blythe was organising in West Limerick. His service record, held in the Irish Military Archives, reveals a remarkably active participant in the struggle: a man who burned barracks, escorted the country's most wanted IRA officers across county lines, was captured and interned at Ballykinlar, and yet returned quietly to his elderly father's farm when it was over. The file contains Daniel's own letters and sworn testimony — his voice across nine decades, direct and unadorned. Like David O'Connor in the O'Connor family (Section 3), Daniel Curtin was awarded a Military Service Pension — a remarkable parallel across the two families whose descendants would marry in 1961.

Military Service Pension — Daniel M. Curtin · Irish Military Archives, File MSP34REF344
Archive Ref.
MSP34REF344 / Associated file 34D92. Military Service Pensions Act, 1934. File dates: 19 November 1934 – 29 August 1935. Irish Military Archives.
Unit
Tournafulla Company, 2nd Battalion, West Limerick Brigade, IRA. Commanding officers: Maurice Harnett and Thomas Leahy. Battalion Adjutant under James Collins (Brigade Adjutant). Witness to application: Maurice Harnett, Secretary, West Limerick Public Utility Society Ltd., Killaculleen, Tournafulla.
Service credited
Period 6 (1 Apr 1920–31 Mar 1921): ½ of entire period = 1 year. Period 7 (1 Apr 1921–11 Jul 1921): entire period = 1 year. Total: 2 years. All other periods (Easter 1916, 1916–1920, Civil War) returned as nil.
Ranks
First critical date: Battalion Adjutant (held on date of arrest, November 1920). Second critical date: Battalion Adjutant. The Commandant of the Battalion swore that Curtin held the rank of Adjutant for about 2 months — October to November 1920.
Pension Awarded
Grade D. Two (2) years of qualifying service at £10 per year of service = £20 per annum, payable from 1st October 1934. Service Certificate issued 6 July 1935 (signed Daniel M. Curtin, 18 July 1935). Award Certificate issued 20 August 1935. Referee: J.K. O'Connor. Department of Justice record: "No record of sentence" — Daniel was an internee, not a convicted prisoner.
Previous Claims
Daniel had previously applied under the Army Pensions Acts 1923, 1927, and 1932, and the Military Service Pensions Act 1924 — all returned nil. His successful claim came only under the Military Service Pensions Act 1934, twelve years after the events concerned.
1 · Daniel Curtin's First Contact — To the Secretary, Dept. of Defence · 19 November 1934
The file begins with Daniel's very first contact with the Department — a brief handwritten note in his neatest hand, written just weeks after the Military Service Pensions Act 1934 came into force:

"Dear Sir, I beg to apply for an Application Form, to qualify for a Certificate of Military Service under the Military Service Pensions Bill 1934. I am Sir, Your obedient servant, Daniel M. Curtin, Killaculleen, Tournafulla, Co. Limk."

Addressed to: The Secretary, Dept. of Defence, Park Gate St., Dublin. Received 20 November 1934. Form issued 29 November 1934. It is the simplest document in the file — a man of nearly fifty, reaching back fifteen years to a conflict he had never publicly spoken about, asking quietly for recognition.
2 · Daniel Curtin's Application Letter — To the Minister for Defence · December 1934
With his completed application form, Daniel submitted a personal letter in his own hand from Killaculleen. It is one of the most direct documents in the family archive:

"I joined the I.R.A. in 1915 as Lieutenant of my Coy at Tournafulla. When Ernest Blythe was organising here in West Limerick and formed our Coy I was elected Lieut by the votes of the men. I did a lot of work drilling and training and collecting arms under terrible difficulties. Collected funds to equip Coy with the other officers and men to the best of my ability. Carried out all exercises, manoeuvres as required. Carried arms openly in presence of Police. Made all preparations as far as I was able to be ready for the expected fight in 1916.

I was arrested at end of 1920 by Auxiliaries & R.I.C. owing to documents captured on a Raid by military previous to this when another member of our Coy was arrested. They found definite information about me in these papers and consequently I was a marked man."

Signed: Daniel M. Curtin, Killaculleen, Tournafulla, Co. Limk. Witnessed by Maurice Harnett.
3 · Daniel Curtin's Sworn Statement — Advisory Committee · 17 May 1935
Six months later, Daniel gave sworn testimony before the Advisory Committee in person. The transcript records his answers directly:

On Easter Week 1916: "You mobilised in Easter Week? Yes. Nothing doing? No."

On the War of Independence: "Attack on Brosna Barracks? I was on out-post duty. I had a Company of men outside." / "Ambush at Abbeyfeale — were you on the attack on the patrol? I was. What were you armed with? A shotgun and revolver."

On his arrest: "You were arrested in Nov. '20? I was. I was caught at home. I was away from home because they raided for me twice. I called to my home for underclothes and had just arrived when I was caught. Who arrested you? The military. Did they charge you with anything? No."

On his rank: "Did you hold any rank? I was Battn. Adjutant. When were you appointed? When John Aherne was arrested in Sept. '20. I was arrested then in Nov. I was adjutant for two months. Who was Battn. Adjutant after you? Tom Leahy. He was arrested too? He was."

On the Civil War: "You took no part in the Civil War? No."
4 · Daniel Curtin's Supplementary Written Statement
Daniel also submitted a further handwritten statement giving additional operational detail — specific operations he had not been asked about in the oral hearing:

"Waited at Devon Rd. Templeglantine in Summer of 1920 for R.I.C. Patrol who were cycling from Abbeyfeale to Strand Newcastle W. There was about half-dozen of us in charge of M. Harnett & Tom Leahy armed with shotguns & revolvers. After waiting for several hours up to eleven at night we withdrew as the Police never came."

"Prepared an ambush for military Cycling Patrol from Newcastle W. to Abbeyfeale of about twenty men at Barna Railway Bridge in August 1920. Arrangements made by our Battalion Council. This was cancelled during the night by orders of Brigade O/C."

"Arrested by Auxiliaries the night I paid a visit home after being raided previously for on two other occasions earlier or a month previously."

"After my release in Dec. 1921 I was placed on the list of unattached officers, but attended meetings and parades of Volunteers and helped generally in their Training as I had additional military knowledge acquired at Ballykinlar Internment Camp."

"At the start of Civil War in July 1922, I had to remain at home, as my Father was dependent on me as he was an old man. But I helped in sheltering the I.R.A. and gave all information to them about the Free State army movements in the locality."

Signed: D.M. Curtin, Tournafulla, Co. Limk.
5 · Maurice Hartnett's Sworn Statement on Behalf of Daniel · 17 May 1935
Maurice Hartnett — Daniel's commanding officer, neighbour in Tournafulla, and witness to his original application — gave sworn corroboration before the Committee on the same day:

"He was Lt. of the local Company in Tournafulla and he was Battn. Adjutant for about a month — from Oct. to Nov. '20."

"Did he go round guarding Dan Breen? Yes, he went to Kerry with us."

"Was he in Abbeyfeale? He was."

"He was on the run? He was for a small bit."

"Up to the time he was arrested he was a good fellow? He was."
Historical Note — Dan Breen, Seán Treacy & the Opening of the War of Independence

To understand why Maurice Hartnett's single line — "Yes, he went to Kerry with us" — carries such weight, it is necessary to know who Dan Breen was.

Dan Breen (b.1894, Donohill, Co. Tipperary; d.1969) and his comrades Seán Treacy, Séamus Robinson and Seán Hogan fired the opening shots of the Irish War of Independence. On 21 January 1919 — the same day the First Dáil met in the Mansion House in Dublin — these four men, with four others, ambushed and killed two RIC constables at Soloheadbeg, Co. Tipperary, while seizing a consignment of gelignite. Breen himself fired first. It was an unauthorised operation, carried out without GHQ sanction, and it started a war.

After Soloheadbeg, all four men were the most wanted in Ireland. The British placed prices on their heads. They survived by moving constantly between trusted safe houses and IRA companies across Munster — relying on men like Maurice Hartnett and Daniel Curtin to keep them alive, armed, and moving across county lines.

Seán Treacy was killed in a gun battle on Talbot Street, Dublin on 14 October 1920 — just weeks before Daniel Curtin himself was captured in November 1920. Dan Breen was wounded multiple times during the war, survived, and later served as a Fianna Fáil TD. He wrote My Fight for Irish Freedom (1924), one of the most widely read memoirs of the period.

Daniel's role was not passive. Hartnett confirms he was armed — "a shotgun and revolver" — and went to Kerry with the escort party. Moving the four most-wanted men in Ireland through territory patrolled by British military and RIC required nerve, local knowledge, and complete trustworthiness. In his sworn statement in May 1935, Daniel listed it simply — one item among others, without emphasis. He was that kind of man. By then, Treacy had been dead fifteen years, Breen was a public figure, and Daniel Curtin was a farmer in Tournafulla who had gone quietly back to his elderly father's land in 1922. The Military Archives file is the only testimony he ever gave.
6 · Internal Military Archives Memo — Adjutants of the 2nd Battalion, West Limerick Brigade
A typed internal memo in the file confirms the sequence of Battalion Adjutants, placing Daniel precisely in the chain of command:

John Aherne, Tournafulla — Adjutant until his capture in October 1920.
Daniel M. Curtin, Tournafulla — Adjutant from October 1920 until his capture by enemy in November 1920.
Thomas Leahy, Tournafulla — then appointed, captured by enemy in March 1921.
Aeneas Sheahan — then appointed, died in September 1921.
Martin Ward — Assistant Acting Adjutant 11 July 1921, acting Adjutant until Sheahan's death, then appointed Adjutant until October 1922.

The memo notes: "It is observed that the Battalion has secured Grade D for three of its Adjutants."
Two Veterans — One Family

By a striking parallel, both of Noreen Curtin's parents' generation included IRA veterans who received Military Service Pensions. Daniel Curtin (Noreen's father) served with the West Limerick Brigade as Battalion Adjutant, was captured by Auxiliaries and interned at Ballykinlar, and was awarded a Grade D pension in 1935. David O'Connor (father of Alice O'Connor, who married into the O'Keeffe family — Section 3) served with the Scartaglin Company, Kerry II Brigade, served in an Active Service Unit, was interned in 1923, and was awarded a Grade E pension in 1934.

Both men were in their early thirties during the most intense period of the conflict. Both were captured. Both returned quietly to farming — Daniel because his elderly father was dependent on him; David because the cause was finished. Both applied for pensions over a decade later, giving sworn testimony before advisory committees. Neither family spoke publicly about the service. The Military Archives files are now the primary testimony to what they did.

The daughter of one — Noreen Curtin, Daniel's daughter — and the grandson of the other — Gerard O'Keeffe, Alice O'Connor's son, and through her David O'Connor's grandson — are connected by marriage. Their children are Katie and Ger O'Keeffe.
Children of Daniel Curtin & Bridget (Babe) Kissane
NameBornDiedNotes
Joan Curtin1926, Killaculleen2012, Killeedy, Ballagh, aged 85–86Married John Hennessy, 1956, Holy Trinity Abbey Church, Adare, Co. Limerick. Buried Killeedy Cemetery.
Maurice Curtin1928, Killaculleen2003, Virginia, Co. Cavan, aged 74–75Married Eleanor Weir, 1959, Mullingar Church. Buried Walshestown Cemetery, Mullingar.
Sean Curtin1929, Killaculleen2010, Killaculleen, aged 80–81Married Eily (Ellen Mary) Hartnett, 1959, Limerick. Buried Tournafulla Cemetery.
Laurence (Larry) Curtin1930, Killaculleen2025, Ballycommane, TournafullaMarried Margaret (Margie) Curtin (daughter of John Francis Curtin & Mary O'Shaughnessy), 1959, St. Joseph's Church, O'Connell Ave, Limerick.
Jimmy Curtin1931, Killaculleen2018, Egmont, Churchtown, Co. CorkMarried Kathleen O'Connor (daughter of Thomas O'Connor & Johanna O'Dwyer), 1961, Church of St. Ita, Feenagh, Co. Limerick. Buried St. Bridget's Cemetery, Churchtown.
Noreen Curtin1939, KillaculleenLivingMarried Michael Curtin (son of William W. Curtin & Mary O'Sullivan, Glenquin South), 1961, Our Lady of Lourdes Church, Co. Limerick. Mother of Catriona (Katie) Curtin.
Chronological Timeline — Curtin Family, Noreen's Line
  • c.1820
    Laurence John Curtin bornBarna, Co. Limerick. He and his wife Johanna O'Brien are the earliest confirmed generation of this Curtin line — great-great-grandparents of Catriona (Katie) Curtin.
  • 1851
    Maurice Laurence Curtin bornKillaculleen, Tournafulla, Co. Limerick. Son of Laurence John Curtin & Johanna O'Brien.
  • 1877
    Maurice Curtin marries Johanna BegleyChurch of Our Lady of the Visitation, Monagea, Co. Limerick. Johanna (b.1853, Monagea; daughter of Cornelius (Con) Begley). Witnesses: Lawrence L Curtin & Thomas Roche.
    Source: Civil marriage certificate, Monagea, Co. Limerick
  • 1878–1885
    Six children born to Maurice & Johanna at KillaculleenJohanna/Joe (1878), Laurence/Larry (1879), Cornelius (1880), John/Jack (1881), Jeremiah/Jerome (1883), Daniel (1885) — father of Noreen Curtin.
  • 1885
    Daniel Curtin bornKillaculleen, Tournafulla, Co. Limerick. Youngest son of Maurice Curtin & Johanna Begley. Father of Noreen Curtin and grandfather of Catriona (Katie) Curtin.
  • 1886
    Johanna Begley Curtin diesKillaculleen, aged 33. Left six young children — Daniel was an infant. Maurice was widowed with children aged from infant to approximately 8 years old.
    Source: Civil death certificate, Killaculleen 1886
  • 1899
    Maurice Curtin marries Margaret O'MahonyAshford Church, Co. Limerick. Second marriage. Margaret was daughter of Thomas O'Mahony, born in Ashford, Co. Limerick.
  • 1915
    Daniel Curtin joins Tournafulla Company, Irish VolunteersKillaculleen, Tournafulla, Co. Limerick. Daniel would serve through the War of Independence, rising to Battalion Adjutant of the 2nd Battalion, West Limerick Brigade.
  • Easter 1916
    Daniel Curtin mobilised at Glenquin CastleUnder the command of Patrick McEnery and Charles Wall. Daniel was sent home after the countermanding order. No active service credited for this period.
  • 1916–1919
    Daniel Curtin — drilling, training and anti-conscription workUnder the command of Maurice Harnett and Dr Edmond Hartnett. Daniel was involved in drilling, training, collecting arms, fundraising, anti-conscription work and scuffles with police at Mount Collins and Tournafulla.
  • 1920–1921
    Daniel Curtin — active service, War of IndependenceCompany Adjutant and First Lieutenant, Tournafulla Company, 2nd Battalion, West Limerick Brigade. Daniel's operations included: burning of Tournafulla Barracks; escorting Dan Breen, Seán Treacy, Séamus Robinson and Seán Hogan from West Limerick to East Kerry; ambush at Abbeyfeale; outpost duty at Brosna RIC Barracks, Co. Kerry; operations at Templeglantine and Barna Railway Bridge.
    Source: Sworn statement, Daniel M. Curtin, Advisory Committee, 17 May 1935; Irish Military Archives MSP34REF344
  • Sep 1920
    Daniel Curtin promoted to Battalion Adjutant2nd Battalion, West Limerick Brigade, following the arrest of John Aherne. Daniel served under the command of James Collins (Brigade Adjutant).
  • Nov 1920
    Daniel Curtin captured — interned at BallykinlarDaniel was taken by British forces while calling at home. He was interned at Ballykinlar Internment Camp, County Down, where he attended IRA-organised classes in field engineering and musketry. Daniel was released in December 1921 under the general amnesty following the Anglo-Irish Treaty.
    Source: Irish Military Archives MSP34REF344
  • 1922
    Daniel Curtin returns to the family farmDaniel returned home to help his father Maurice on the farm at Killaculleen. During the Civil War he did not claim active service but billeted IRA men on the run and passed on information about the movements of National forces.
  • 1925
    Maurice Laurence Curtin diesKillaculleen, Tournafulla, aged 73–74.
    Source: Civil death certificate, Killaculleen 1925
  • 1925
    Daniel Curtin marries Bridget (Babe) KissaneSt. Mary's Church, Asdee, Ballylongford, Co. Kerry. Daniel (aged ~40) married Bridget Kissane (b.1894, Kilcox, Lisselton, Co. Kerry; daughter of John Richard Kissane & Hanora Buckley). Daniel brought a Kerry woman home to Tournafulla.
    Source: Civil marriage certificate, Ballylongford, Co. Kerry 1925
  • 18 Apr 1926
    1926 Census — Daniel Curtin household, KillaculleenDaniel (head, 40, farmer, 156 acres), Bridget (wife, 31, Co. Kerry), infant daughter Joan. Brother Cornelius (47, single) assisting on farm. Two farm labourers and two female servants also present.
    Source: Census of the Irish Free State 1926, Killaculleen, Schedule 54–5
  • 1926–1931
    Children born to Daniel & Bridget at KillaculleenJoan (1926), Maurice (1928), Sean (1929), Laurence/Larry (1930), Jimmy (1931).
  • 1935
    Daniel Curtin — Military Service Pension awardedGrade D — two years of qualifying service for the War of Independence period (April 1920–July 1921). Daniel's sworn statement was given before the Advisory Committee on 17 May 1935, corroborated by Maurice Harnett.
    Source: Irish Military Archives, MSP34REF344, file dates 19 November 1934 – 29 August 1935
  • 1939
    Bridget (Babe) Kissane Curtin diesKillaculleen, aged 44–45. Left five children, the youngest — Noreen — aged approximately 8 months. Daniel was widowed with young children still at home.
    Source: Civil death certificate, Killaculleen 1939
  • 1939
    Noreen Curtin bornKillaculleen, Tournafulla, Co. Limerick. Youngest child of Daniel Curtin & Bridget Kissane. Mother of Catriona (Katie) Curtin.
  • 1946
    Daniel Curtin diesKillaculleen. Died before 30 September 1946 — confirmed by a returned pension payment envelope stamped "Deceased," postmarked Baile Átha Cliath 30 Sep 1946, found in his Military Archives file. Buried Old Churchtown Cemetery, Newcastlewest, Co. Limerick.
    Source: Irish Military Archives, MSP34REF344 (death confirmed by returned pension envelope, postmarked 30 September 1946)
  • 1961
    Noreen Curtin marries Michael CurtinOur Lady of Lourdes Church, Co. Limerick. Noreen (b.1939, Killaculleen) married Michael Curtin (b.1931, Glenquin South, Tournafulla; son of William W. Curtin & Mary O'Sullivan). Both sides of Katie's family carry the Curtin surname but from different townlands within Tournafulla parish.
    Source: Civil marriage certificate, Co. Limerick 1961
  • 1962–1969
    Four children born to Michael & Noreen Curtin, Glenquin SouthMary (1962), William (1964), Breda (1966), Catriona/Katie (1969) — wife of Gerard (Ger) O'Keeffe.
Noreen Curtin & Michael Curtin — Marriage 1961
Marriage — Noreen Curtin & Michael Curtin · 1961
Location
Our Lady of Lourdes Church, Co. Limerick, Ireland
Bride
Noreen Curtin — born 1939, Killaculleen, Tournafulla, Co. Limerick. Daughter of Daniel Curtin & Bridget (Babe) Kissane.
Groom
Michael Curtin — born 1931, Glenquin South, Tournafulla, Co. Limerick. Son of William W. Curtin & Mary O'Sullivan. (See Section 6.)
Children
Mary (b.1962), William (b.1964), Breda (b.1966), Katrina/Catriona (Katie) (b.1969) — all born Glenquin South, Tournafulla, Co. Limerick.
Section 6
The Curtin Family
Meenahela & Glenquin South, Tournafulla, Co. Limerick · 1772 to present

This section documents the Curtin family of Meenahela and Glenquin South, Tournafulla, Co. Limerick — the paternal family of Catriona (Katie) Curtin. The handwritten family tree and gravestone at Meenahela Cemetery now allow us to trace this line back to Michael Curtin (1772–1834) — two full generations earlier than previously recorded, and a century before the 1926 census. William P. Curtin (1841–1922) and his wife Julia McCarthy are Katie's great-great-grandparents. Their son William W. Curtin and Mary O'Sullivan are her paternal grandparents, and their son Michael Curtin (b.1931; d.2015) married Noreen Curtin of Killaculleen in 1961. The Curtin family came originally from Ballycommane, Co. Limerick.

Generation Tree · Section 6
Origins — Michael Curtin (1772–1834) & the Ballycommane Line

The handwritten Curtin family tree, compiled by a family member and confirmed by the Meenahela gravestone, allows this line to be traced back to Michael Curtin (1772–1834) — Katie's great-great-great-grandfather. The family originated in Ballycommane, Co. Limerick. Michael Curtin had at least three children: Aurora, Con, and Patrick. Patrick (noted as "my great great Grandfather, came from Ballycommane") married a Joan Fitzgerald of the Light House, Dromtrasna. Their son William P. Curtin (1841–1922) married Julia McCarthy of Dromtrasna, and it is William P. Curtin who is commemorated on the Meenahela gravestone.

Gravestone — William P. Curtin · Meenahela Cemetery, Tournafulla, Co. Limerick
Memorial
Sacred to the memory of William P. Curtin, born September 1841, died 22 April 1922. Erected by his wife Julia and family, Meenahela.
Also commemorated
His wife Julia — d. November 1939
Their son Patrick — d. 26 April 1933
His daughter Bridget — d. 12 March 1949
Mary Curtin (Mary O'Sullivan, wife of William W. Curtin) — d. 2 January 1950
William Curtin (William W. Curtin, son) — d. 19 October 1977
Bridget P. Curtin — d. 8 December 1980
William P. Curtin (grandson) — d. 30 July 2001
Michael P. Curtin (Katie's father) — d. 12 January 2008
Location
Meenahela Cemetery, Tournafulla, Co. Limerick. The family home was at Meenahela — confirmed by the gravestone inscription "erected by his wife Julia and family, Meenahela."
Note on Michael Curtin's death date
The gravestone records Michael P. Curtin's death as 12 January 2008. Previous records in this document cited 2015 — the gravestone is treated as the definitive source and all references have been corrected accordingly.
The Curtin Family Tree — William P. Curtin & Julia McCarthy · Children
The handwritten family tree records the following children of William P. Curtin (1841–1922) and Julia McCarthy of Dromtrasna:

Patrick Curtin — died young; "eldest in family, also Patrick father." Married Bridget O'Connor. Children: Eily and M. Curtin.
Nora Curtin — became a Nun.
Hannah Curtin — married ML Cotter. Daughter: Sheila (single).
James Curtin — "worked in Con's house." Married Nora Ahern, Dublin. Daughter: Maureen (Nun).
Michael Curtin — went to USA. Became a priest (Fr).
William Curtin — "came from Meenahela, my grandfather, died 1977" (confirmed by gravestone: d. 19 October 1977). Married Mary O'Sullivan (d. 2 January 1950, also on gravestone). Son: Michael Curtin — Katie's father.
Johanna Curtin — became a Nun. Children: Patrick and Hilary.
Tim Curtin — went to USA.
Source: Handwritten Curtin family tree, family collection; Meenahela gravestone, Tournafulla, Co. Limerick
William W. Curtin & Mary O'Sullivan
William W. Curtin
Born c.1890, Abbeydorney South, Co. Kerry · Died 19 October 1977, Meenahela · Buried Meenahela Cemetery
Parents
William P. Curtin (1841–1922) & Julia McCarthy, Meenahela, Tournafulla, Co. Limerick.
Role
Paternal grandfather of Catriona (Katie) Curtin. Son of William P. Curtin (1841–1922). Came to Glenquin South as Creamery Manager. Death date confirmed by Meenahela gravestone: 19 October 1977.
Mary O'Sullivan
Born c.1889, Mountenane, Co. Limerick · Died 2 January 1950, Meenahela · Buried Meenahela Cemetery
Parents
Daughter of the O'Sullivan family, Mountenane. Her mother Catherine Sullivan (b.c.1861, Rahenagh) is recorded as a widow in the 1926 census.
Note
Death date confirmed by Meenahela gravestone: 2 January 1950. She died when Michael Curtin (her son, Katie's father) was approximately 19 years old.
Michael Curtin
Born 8 March 1931, Glenquin South · Died 12 January 2008, Meenahela · Buried Meenahela Cemetery
Parents
William W. Curtin & Mary O'Sullivan, Glenquin South, Tournafulla.
Marriage
Married Noreen Curtin (daughter of Daniel Curtin & Bridget Kissane, Killaculleen) in 1961 at Our Lady of Lourdes Church, Co. Limerick. Father of Catriona (Katie) Curtin. Death date confirmed by Meenahela gravestone: 12 January 2008.

Glenquin South, Tournafulla · c.1970–1971
Three Generations · c.1970–1971
The Curtin Family
Glenquin South, Tournafulla
Seated: William W. Curtin (b.c.1890, d.19 Oct 1977) — Katie's grandfather, son of William P. Curtin of Meenahela. Aged approximately 80 at the time of this photograph. Baby Catriona (Katie) Curtin on his lap, aged approximately 1–2 years.

Standing left: Michael Curtin (b.8 Mar 1931, d.12 Jan 2008) — Katie's father, son of William W. Curtin & Mary O'Sullivan.

Standing behind: Identity to be confirmed.
Three generations of the Curtin family of Glenquin South, Tournafulla — grandfather, father, and daughter — photographed at the family home. William W. Curtin died in October 1977, six years after this photograph was likely taken.
Chronological Timeline — Curtin Family, Michael's Line
  • 1772
    Michael Curtin bornBallycommane, Co. Limerick. Katie's great-great-great-grandfather. The earliest confirmed member of this Curtin line. The family originated in Ballycommane — noted on the handwritten family tree by a family member.
    Source: Handwritten Curtin family tree, family collection
  • 1834
    Michael Curtin diesAged approximately 62. His children included Patrick (who married Joan Fitzgerald of Dromtrasna), Con, and Aurora.
  • Sep 1841
    William P. Curtin bornMeenahela, Tournafulla, Co. Limerick. Son of Patrick Curtin & Joan Fitzgerald. Katie's great-great-grandfather. Birth confirmed by Meenahela gravestone.
    Source: Meenahela gravestone, Tournafulla, Co. Limerick
  • c.1870s
    William P. Curtin marries Julia McCarthyJulia McCarthy came from Dromtrasna, Meenahela area. They settled at Meenahela, Tournafulla, Co. Limerick and had at least eight children.
  • c.1880s–1890s
    Children born to William P. Curtin & Julia McCarthy, MeenahelaPatrick, Nora, Hannah, James, Michael (USA, priest), William W. Curtin (Katie's grandfather), Johanna, Tim (USA).
    Source: Handwritten Curtin family tree, family collection
  • c.1889
    Mary O'Sullivan bornMountenane, Co. Limerick. Daughter of the Sullivan family of the Mountenane/Rahenagh area. Her mother Catherine Sullivan (b.c.1861, Rahenagh) is recorded as a widow in the 1926 census.
  • c.1890
    William W. Curtin bornSon of William P. Curtin & Julia McCarthy, Meenahela, Tournafulla. He later came to Glenquin South as Creamery Manager. His birthplace in the 1926 census is recorded as Abbeydorney South, Co. Kerry — suggesting he trained or worked in Kerry before settling in Limerick.
  • c.1920
    William W. Curtin marries Mary O'SullivanCo. Limerick. William came from Kerry to manage the Glenquin South creamery and married locally. They settled at Glenquin South, Tournafulla.
  • 22 Apr 1922
    William P. Curtin diesMeenahela, Tournafulla, aged 80. Buried Meenahela Cemetery. His wife Julia erected the gravestone.
    Source: Meenahela gravestone, Tournafulla
  • 18 Apr 1926
    1926 Census — William Curtin household, Glenquin SouthWilliam (head, 35, Creamery Manager, 88 acres), Mary (wife, ~36, 4 children born, 3 living), children Lilian (~3), Willie (~1½), Julia (~3 months). Mother-in-law Catherine Sullivan (65, widow) also present.
    Source: Census of the Irish Free State 1926, Glenquin South, Tournafulla
  • 8 Mar 1931
    Michael Curtin bornGlenquin South, Tournafulla, Co. Limerick. Son of William W. Curtin & Mary O'Sullivan. Father of Catriona (Katie) Curtin.
  • Nov 1939
    Julia McCarthy Curtin diesMeenahela. Wife of William P. Curtin. Buried Meenahela Cemetery.
    Source: Meenahela gravestone, Tournafulla
  • 2 Jan 1950
    Mary O'Sullivan Curtin diesMeenahela, aged approximately 60. Wife of William W. Curtin. Buried Meenahela Cemetery. Michael Curtin was approximately 19 when his mother died.
    Source: Meenahela gravestone, Tournafulla
  • 1961
    Michael Curtin marries Noreen CurtinOur Lady of Lourdes Church, Co. Limerick. Michael (b.1931, Glenquin South) married Noreen Curtin (b.1939, Killaculleen; daughter of Daniel Curtin & Bridget Kissane). Both families from within Tournafulla parish, both carrying the Curtin name from different lines.
    Source: Civil marriage certificate, Co. Limerick 1961
  • 1962–1969
    Four children born to Michael & Noreen Curtin, Glenquin SouthMary (1962), William (1964), Breda (1966), Catriona/Katie (1969) — wife of Gerard (Ger) O'Keeffe.
  • 19 Oct 1977
    William W. Curtin diesMeenahela, Tournafulla. Aged approximately 87. Buried Meenahela Cemetery alongside his wife Mary.
    Source: Meenahela gravestone, Tournafulla
  • 12 Jan 2008
    Michael Curtin diesMeenahela, Tournafulla, Co. Limerick. Father of Catriona (Katie) Curtin. Aged 76. Buried Meenahela Cemetery. Note: previously recorded in this document as 2015 — the gravestone is the definitive source.
    Source: Meenahela gravestone, Tournafulla, Co. Limerick
Section 7
Living Generations
The Family Branches Today

The six family sections of this document trace eight family lines across Counties Cork, Kerry and Limerick from the early nineteenth century to the mid-twentieth century. This section carries each branch forward — into the living generations, and the world they inhabit today. It will be built out branch by branch as information is gathered. The first branch documented here is the O'Connor family of San Francisco — the American line of Dave Ned O'Connor and Kate O'Leary, carrying the story from Dromulton, Scartaglin to California across three generations.

The O'Connor Family — San Francisco, USA

Jeremiah O'Connor — son of Dave Ned O'Connor and Kate O'Leary — emigrated from Mount, Scartaglin to San Francisco in approximately 1964, married Anna there in 1966, and raised four children. Both Jeremiah and Anna are deceased. Their full record is documented in Section 3. This section covers the living generation — their son David O'Connor and his children, who are the current generation of the O'Connor family in San Francisco. The line from Dromulton, Scartaglin to San Francisco now spans five generations.

The O'Connor line from Dromulton to San Francisco:
John O'Connor & Hanora Browne (Dromulton) → Edmond O'Connor & Bridget Brosnan → David (Dave Ned) O'Connor & Kate O'Leary (Scartaglin, 1925) → Jeremiah O'Connor (b.1933, San Francisco) → David O'Connor (b.1973) → Aidan & Jack O'Connor (b.2002, San Francisco)
David O'Connor & Sons — San Francisco
Archbishop Riordan High School · San Francisco · c.2019
David O'Connor & Sons
David O'Connor
with Aidan James & Jack Michael
David O'Connor (b. 30 March 1973) is the son of Jeremiah O'Connor and grandson of David (Dave Ned) O'Connor & Kate O'Leary of Mount, Scartaglin. He is the great-great-grandson of John O'Connor & Hanora Browne of Dromulton.
Aidan James O'Connor — b. 23 July 2002, San Francisco
Jack Michael O'Connor — b. 23 July 2002, San Francisco

Aidan and Jack are pictured here in the colours of Archbishop Riordan High School — a Marianist school in San Francisco with strong Irish-American ties. They are fifth-generation descendants of the O'Connor family of Dromulton, Scartaglin, Co. Kerry.
Kathleen O'Connor & Patrick Hanley
Children of Kathleen O'Connor & Patrick Hanley
Kathleen O'Connor (b. 10 April 1968), eldest daughter of Jeremiah & Anna, married Patrick Hanley. Their children are:

Ryan Connor Hanley — b. 18 November 1995
Emily Anne Hanley — b. 4 November 1997
Anna Maria O'Connor — In Memoriam
Anna Maria O'Connor · 30 January 1977 – 26 June 2019
Anna Maria O'Connor, youngest child of Jeremiah & Anna, was born in San Francisco on 30 January 1977 and died on 26 June 2019, aged 42. She is the mother of Ronan Anthony Wiles (b. 28 May 2012), who carries the Wiles surname from his father. She is remembered with love by her family. RIP.
O'Connor Family — San Francisco · Summary Table
O'Connor Family — San Francisco Branch · Three Generations
NameBornDiedNotes
Jeremiah O'Connor9 Jul 19337 Apr 2003Son of Dave Ned O'Connor & Kate O'Leary, Scartaglin. Emigrated to San Francisco. Married Anna, 14 May 1966.
Anna O'Connor19 Jun 193220 Jan 2003Wife of Jeremiah O'Connor, San Francisco.

Ronan Anthony Wiles28 May 2012—Son of Anna Maria O'Connor & Wiles, San Francisco. Grandson of Jeremiah & Anna O'Connor.
From Dromulton to San Francisco — Five Generations

The journey of this branch of the O'Connor family spans five generations and two continents. John O'Connor farmed at Dromulton, below the church at Scartaglin, in the mid-nineteenth century. His grandson Dave Ned O'Connor fought in the War of Independence, married Kate O'Leary of Mount Scartaglin in 1925, and spent his life on Kerry farmland. His son Jeremiah crossed the Atlantic and made his life in San Francisco. Jeremiah's son David was born there in 1973. David's sons — Aidan and Jack — were born in 2002, photographed here in the purple and gold of Archbishop Riordan High School, a world away from Scartaglin and yet carrying the same O'Connor name that appears on the 1901 census at Dromulton.

They are the fifth generation of this family in direct line from Dromulton. The farm is gone. The language is different. The country is different. But the line is unbroken.
The Curtin Family · Ireland — Living Generations

Michael Curtin (b. 8 March 1931, Glenquin South) and Noreen Curtin (b. 1939, Killaculleen) had four children: Mary (b. 1962), William (Willie) (b. 1964), Breda (b. 1966), and Catriona (Katie) (b. 1969) — who married David Gerard (Ger) O'Keeffe. Both Willie and Breda have married and raised families in Ireland, carrying the Curtin name and connection to Tournafulla, Co. Limerick into the present generation.

Breda Curtin & Patrick Hayes
Marriage · 19 June 1992
Breda Curtin (b. 1966), daughter of Michael & Noreen Curtin of Glenquin South, Tournafulla, married Patrick Hayes on 19 June 1992. Breda is the elder sister of Catriona (Katie) Curtin — wife of David Gerard (Ger) O'Keeffe — and is thus a direct link between the Curtin family of Sections 5 & 6 and the living generation. Their five children are:

Kate Hayes — b. 20 February 1995
Megan Hayes — b. 15 October 1996
Anna Hayes — b. 20 August 1998
Mike Hayes — b. 15 August 2000
Sarah Hayes — b. 23 May 2006
William (Willie) Curtin & Ursula Myles

William (Willie) Curtin (b. 1964), son of Michael & Noreen Curtin of Glenquin South, and brother of Catriona (Katie) Curtin, married Ursula Myles on 25 September 1995. Their three children — Alan, Mary, and Andrew — are grandchildren of Michael & Noreen Curtin and great-grandchildren of William W. Curtin & Mary O'Sullivan of Glenquin South, from whom the Sections 5 & 6 Curtin lines descend.

Alan Curtin · b. 23 September 1998
Willie Curtin & Ursula Myles
Alan Curtin
b. 23 September 1998
Alan Curtin is the eldest son of William (Willie) Curtin and Ursula Myles of Tournafulla, Co. Limerick. He is a grandson of Michael & Noreen Curtin of Glenquin South, and a great-grandson of William W. Curtin & Mary O'Sullivan.
Alan is a fourth-generation descendant of the Curtin family of Glenquin South, Tournafulla — the line traced in full in Section 6 of this record.
Andrew Curtin · b. 15 May 2002
Willie Curtin & Ursula Myles
Andrew Curtin
b. 15 May 2002
Andrew Curtin is the youngest son of William (Willie) Curtin and Ursula Myles of Tournafulla, Co. Limerick. He is a grandson of Michael & Noreen Curtin of Glenquin South, and a great-grandson of William W. Curtin & Mary O'Sullivan.
Andrew is a fourth-generation descendant of the Curtin family of Glenquin South, Tournafulla — the line traced in full in Section 6 of this record.
Children of William (Willie) Curtin & Ursula Myles
Willie Curtin (b. 1964) and Ursula Myles married on 25 September 1995. Their three children are:

Alan Curtin — b. 23 September 1998
Mary Curtin — b. 29 December 1999
Andrew Curtin — b. 15 May 2002

Alan and Andrew are pictured here. They are grandchildren of Michael & Noreen Curtin of Glenquin South, great-grandchildren of William W. Curtin (Creamery Manager) & Mary O'Sullivan, and great-great-grandchildren of William P. Curtin of Meenahela — the line traced in full in Section 6.

From Tournafulla to the Present Generation

The Curtin children of Glenquin South — Mary, Willie, Breda and Katie — represent the fourth generation of the Curtin line traced in Sections 5 and 6. Their children and grandchildren carry forward a line that reaches back through Michael Curtin (b. 1931), William W. Curtin (the Creamery Manager), William P. Curtin of Meenahela (b. 1841), and ultimately to Michael Curtin of Ballycommane (b. 1772) — more than two centuries of this family in County Limerick. The Curtin name is alive and well.
Appendix
How the Families Connect
How the Families Connect
How Sections 1–4 Come Together
Sections 1 & 2
Eugene O'Keeffe (O'Keeffe family, Section 1) married Helena Fitzgerald (Fitzgerald & Reen family, Section 2) on 24 November 1925 at Rathmore, Co. Kerry. They lived at Lake Vale, Ballydesmond, Co. Cork and had five children: Sheila, William, Nellie, Joan, and Tom (Fr.).
Sections 3 & 4
David O'Connor (O'Connor family, Section 3) married Kate O'Leary (O'Leary family, Section 4) around 1925 at Scartaglin, Co. Kerry. They lived at Farrankeal, Currow and had several children including Alice (born Tralee, 1931).
All Four Sections
William O'Keeffe (son of Eugene & Helena) married Alice O'Connor (daughter of David & Kate) on 25 February 1965 at St. Stephens Church, Castleisland, Co. Kerry. Their children include David Gerard (Ger) O'Keeffe (born 23 July 1969).
Sections 5 & 6
Noreen Curtin (Killaculleen, Tournafulla — Section 5) married Michael Curtin (Glenquin South, Tournafulla — Section 6) in 1961 at Our Lady of Lourdes Church, Co. Limerick. Their daughter Catriona (Katie) Curtin (b.1969, Glenquin South) married David Gerard (Ger) O'Keeffe (b.23 July 1969, Cork — son of William O'Keeffe & Alice O'Connor, from Sections 1–4).
Section 7
To be added — further family branches to be documented.

O'Leary, Castleisland Registrar's District, 4 February 1925 · Séamus Fleming, fkancestry.com — O'Leary Mount Scartaglin & O'Connor Dromultan research (updated 01/06/2023) · Scanlon family census records, Gneeves, Brosna, Kerry 1901 & 1911 · O'Leary rent book, Mount Scartaglin 1852–1876 (family collection) · Descendants of Laurence John Curtin (MyHeritage family tree report, compiled 2024) · Irish Military Archives, Military Service Pension file Daniel M. Curtin MSP34REF344 (1934–1935) · 1926 Census: Killaculleen (Schedule 54–5) & Glenquin South, Tournafulla, Co. Limerick. All primary documents held in family collection.
